reviews.133246:
This 7:24 cut of Erotic City (Make Love Not War Erotic City Come Alive) might be my favourite Prince song. A B-side from the Let's Go Crazy single from Purple Rain, it's a straight Minneapolis funk classic. 

Prince said that he recorded it directly after seeing Parliament-Funkadelic at the Beverly Theatre in Los Angeles in 1983. The song also features the recorded debut of Sheila E on a Prince song.

Put it on in the kitchen and try not to dance. See how long you last.

Standout track: Erotic City (Make Love Not War Erotic City Come Alive)
reviews.189076:
Great album, great vocals, love the personality of the Jones Girls. 'Who Can I Run To' is a lovely song that may be familiar to fans of John Early.

Standout track: You Gonna Make Me Love Somebody Else
reviews.480387:
Paul Simon's next release after the incredibly successful Graceland, this record takes influence from Brazil rather than Africa. 'The Obvious Child' is such a great opening track. A bit of an underrated album, probably very overshadowed by its predecessor. 

Standout track: Can't Run But
reviews.681612: A great film and great soundtrack. Michael Mann gets a cold classic from Tangerine Dream for his 1981 feature film debut.
reviews.716065:
Bob James, the most sampled man in hip-hop for a reason! Really love this instrumental jazz fusion, and his other albums from around the same period are all worth a listen (Head and BJ4 in particular).

Standout track: Angela (Theme from 'Taxi')
reviews.2130952:
God, this album goes so hard. These guys are just so incredibly locked in. James Cotton, who got his start as Muddy Waters' harp player, is completely on fire here. Album opener 'Boogie Thing' is a complete scorcher. I did not know that the baritone sax player on this album was named Lenny "The Sensous King" Baker until just now.

Standout track: Boogie Thing
reviews.3137330:
One of my favourite live recordings, it really captures the feeling of being in the room (which I really wish I was). It features what is (in my opinion) Cooder's prime lineup with background singers Bobby King, Eldridge King and Terry Evans, and accordion player Flaco Jimenez. It's a wonderful moment when Cooder steps back and lets King, King & Evans shine on the cover of The Dark End of the Street.

Standout track: Jesus on the Mainline
reviews.3254503:
I love this album so much, this was the moment that their disparate influences cohered into something great that was all theirs. Great harmonies, interesting time signatures, insane falsetto. It's pretty much wall to wall bangers.

Standout track: Love You Inside Out
reviews.3733751:
Originally intended as the soundtrack for the movie Drive, Johnny Jewel repurposed the album into this debut release from Symmetry. Cool, cold, dreamlike and cinematic. 

Standout track: The Messenger
reviews.4864607:
Three of the best voices of their generation, I only wish they'd been able to record together in the 70s when they first met. Beautiful effortless harmonies, the best session musicians around and a guitar solo from Ry Cooder in there. What's not to like?

Standout track: Telling Me Lies
reviews.5267354:
Before he scored movies, Randy Newman was writing unusual, sardonic songs.

More often than not, his songs from this period are about the rot lurking below the surface of American life. Sail Away kills me every time.

Standout track: Sail Away
reviews.6392963:
I've been really enjoying these Windham Hill releases since finding a copy of Mark Isham's Vapor Drawings and buying it based off the intriguing cover art. Isham is a multi-instrumentalist and composer, having also scored many films and TV shows (including many cult classics like Point Break, The Hitcher, Fire in the Sky, Blade and many others). 

I'm very drawn to this type of instrumental new wave ambient music, and the results are very cinematic. Isham plays all of the instruments here - synthesizers, trumpet, flugelhorn, piano, saxophone, Steiner EVI and electronic percussion - to interesting and varied result.

Standout track: Sympathy And Acknowledgement
reviews.11079090:
Some of the very best production of Muggs' career, dark and hypnotic. Probably my favourite of Cypress Hill's records.

Standout track: Boom Biddy Bye Bye
reviews.11309621:
John Martyn made this album with Phil Collins on the drums and backing vocals as they bonded over their recent respective divorces - and it shows! A mellow and somewhat melancholy album, I love his oddball delivery on 'Some People Are Crazy', and he gives a reggae-inflected track that seemed to be a requirement for all rockers in the late 70s and early 80s.

Standout track: Some People Are Crazy
reviews.12008370:
Man, I love this album and I love Kenny. The dude is just human sunshine. This is his debut solo album after Loggins & Messina, and it opens with a bang. It features Bob James and Richard Tee, two of my favourite composers and session musicians who can be found across so much incredible music from this era. 'Set It Free' builds beautifully across its 5:56 runtime and the arrangements at the end are classic Bob James.

Standout track: Lady Luck
reviews.12134043: Oh you haven't checked out Phill Collins' jazz fusion group he was in when he was worried that Genesis was about to end? You should probably check it out. He's getting up to some wild stuff here.
reviews.13336205:
There are some beautiful songs on here, fantastic session musicians and soulful renditions including Jackon's reworked cover of Merle Haggard's 'If We're Not Back in Love by Monday' (there's a great and wonderfully smooth segue from the preceding track, 'Cheatin' Is'). 

The swinging R&B of A Little Taste of Outside Love' swoops smoothly into the hard righteous funk of 'You Created a Monster'. But if you want to hear Jackson at the height of her powers, you really need to soak up the acid-tongued bitchiness of the 10 minute opener 'All The Way Lover'. 

Standout track: All The Way Lover
reviews.15277229:
10cc have often struck me as being a kind of analogous British alternative universe version of Steely Dan. Absolute studio weapons, songwriting mostly driven by a pair of musicians, with their songwriting and lyrics driven just as much by the ideas of art and cinema as anything else. 

Like Steely Dan, they seemed to stretch the limits of what could fit into the bucket of 'popular rock' - prog-like time signature shifts, jazzy minor chord progression, deeply layered vocal harmonies, sardonic lyrics. 

I can't go past the album closer, the 11:34 opus Feel The Benefit - (Pts. 1, 2 & 3). "Feel the benefit" is stretched from a typical British idiom to pondering about how the artists of the world justify their existence, to dreams of universal harmony. Incredibly lush orchestration, amazing solos, a mid-song Latin break - what more could you want?

Standout track: Feel The Benefit - (Pts. 1, 2 & 3)
reviews.25112611:
Man these two are great. World class new jazz here, if you didn't know better you'd swear those breaks were programmed. Eclectic guest list including the legend Herbie Hancock.

Standout track: Sniff
reviews.30278048:
These guys are so great, I had the pleasure of seeing them headline a night at the Meredith Music Festival and was very surprised by how different the live versions of their material was! If you're interested in a kind of Indian-inflected version of Khruangbin out of Melbourne, give Glass Beams a go.

Standout track: Snake oil
reviews.31793180:
I've been a fan of Photay since I heard his self-titled EP, released on Astro Nautico in 2014. He's continued to develop his experimental electronic sound, with its incorporation of field recordings, synthesisers and live instrumentation. All of his compositions are interesting, organic, original and deeply human.

Standout track: Thermal Loop
